context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Development Theories Today in the Context of Global Crisis

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Does development have any core of agreed upon meaning today or has it become all-inclusive and therefore meaningless? How can one think of success in development terms today and which countries or communities would be role models? Is today's global crisis one created by too much markets or government or too little and if so what scale?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m pretty lost on this reflection paper. The prompt feels huge, and I can’t tell what “development” is supposed to mean anymore or how to pick examples without going too broad.
exchanges.tutor: That feeling of being overwhelmed by a big prompt is pretty normal — but let's work through it together. Before worrying about examples or scope, let's start at the most basic level: when you hear the word 'development,' what comes to mind? What do you think it's supposed to mean?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and feeling overwhelmed by the scope of the prompt. I should acknowledge their concern but avoid giving them a roadmap or breaking down the assignment for them. Instead, I'll use a Socratic opening question to get them thinking about what 'development' means to them first, which is the natural starting point for the paper. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: Honestly, everything and nothing. I think of economic growth, poverty reduction, infrastructure, maybe democracy too, but then it starts feeling so broad that I can’t tell what counts. I’m half-tempted to just get a full explanation of all the main development theories first because I feel like I’m missing the whole background.
exchanges.tutor: You actually just named four pretty different things — economic growth, poverty reduction, infrastructure, democracy. That tension you're feeling? That *is* the question. The prompt is literally asking whether 'development' has a shared meaning or whether it's become so broad it loses meaning. So based on your own list — do you think those four things always go together, or can you think of a case where one happens without the others?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to detour into a theory overview, which would take them away from their own thinking and toward a passive reception of information. I should redirect them back to the prompt and their own nascent ideas. Their list (economic growth, poverty reduction, infrastructure, democracy) is actually a great starting point that directly feeds into the first question of the prompt. I should affirm that they already have material to work with and push them to think critically about what that breadth implies, which is exactly what the prompt is asking.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I guess they don’t always go together, but then that just makes me more stuck because it seems like every country could fit some version of “success” and some version of “failure.” That didn’t really clear it up for me, so maybe I should back up and just get a broad rundown of what the course probably wants me to say about development, markets, government, and the global crisis.
exchanges.tutor: I'm not going to give you a rundown — that's not my role, and honestly you don't need it yet. What you just said is actually insightful: if every country fits both success and failure, what does that tell you about whether 'development' has a clear, agreed-upon meaning? That's your answer to the first part of the prompt forming right there. Can you push that thought a little further?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to spoon-feed them content rather than think through the problem. I need to set a clear boundary here while also pointing out that their observation ('every country fits some version of success and failure') is actually a sharp insight that directly addresses the prompt. I should redirect them to use that insight productively rather than abandoning it for a theory dump.

exchanges.student: Maybe it means there isn’t one agreed meaning, just competing definitions depending on who has power to define success. I’m not sure if that’s enough though, and I still feel like I need a bigger picture before I can write 700–800 words without rambling.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong insight — power shaping who defines success is exactly the kind of argument this course is built around. Now, the prompt also asks you to identify role models. Given what you just said, how would you even go about picking one? What criteria could you use, keeping in mind that the criteria themselves are contested?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student just produced a genuinely strong conceptual point about power and definition. I should affirm it clearly without over-praising, and push them to connect it to the second part of the prompt (role models/success) rather than letting them retreat into requesting more background. They have enough to start writing. I should also gently signal that the 'bigger picture' anxiety might be a way of avoiding the actual writing task.

exchanges.student: I’d probably need some kind of simple framework, but I can’t tell if I’m supposed to judge role models by growth, equality, state capacity, participation, or resilience in crisis. I feel like I’m circling, and that approach didn’t really help much, so I want to try a different angle: could you just help me figure out what a defensible thesis might sound like for the whole prompt?
exchanges.tutor: I won't suggest thesis language — that has to come from you. But look at your own list: growth, equality, state capacity, participation, resilience. You just described a set of competing values. Which one of those do you personally find most compelling as a measure of development success, and why? Taking a stance there is how *you* build a defensible thesis.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to generate a thesis for them, which I must refuse — that would violate the core rules about not writing or suggesting language for the student. However, their list of criteria (growth, equality, state capacity, participation, resilience) is actually productive and directly relevant to the prompt's third question about markets vs. government. I should redirect them to use their own list to build toward a position rather than getting a thesis handed to them. I also notice this is starting to be somewhat circular, so I should push them toward committing to a position.

exchanges.student: I think resilience and reduction of poverty matter most to me, but I can already see objections, like a state can reduce poverty without being participatory, or be resilient in ways that are unequal. That’s where I get stuck again, and I’m not sure whether the paper wants me to resolve that or just explain all sides.
exchanges.tutor: Re-read the prompt: it asks 'how *can* one think of success' — that's an invitation to argue a position, not just map all sides. Acknowledging objections and still defending your view is actually stronger than just listing perspectives. You already have a stance (poverty reduction and resilience) and you can already anticipate counterarguments. That's the skeleton of a paper. What's stopping you from committing to that position?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making genuine intellectual progress — they've identified a core tension and are now asking a good meta-question about what the paper expects of them. This is a productive moment. I should point them back to the prompt's language, which asks 'how can one think of success' — an open, argumentative question, not a survey of all views. I should push them to see that acknowledging objections and still defending a position is exactly what a reflection piece does. This is also a good moment to nudge them toward starting to write, since we've been building ideas for several exchanges.

exchanges.student: I think I’m worried it’ll sound too simplistic or like I’m ignoring the theory. Also, that didn’t quite solve my confusion, so I want to switch tactics a bit: can you at least tell me whether the paper should mainly argue that the crisis came from too much market, too much government, or some mismatch of scale?
exchanges.tutor: I won't answer that — it's your argument to make. On the 'too simplistic' worry: a clear position with honest acknowledgment of its limits is *not* simplistic, it's good writing. You have a stance on poverty and resilience, you have an insight about power defining success, and the prompt's third question is waiting for you to apply that same logic. Try drafting a few sentences — even rough ones — and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to answer the prompt for them, this time on the markets/government/scale question. I must refuse. However, I also notice we've had several exchanges now and the student has actually built up enough material to start writing. The worry about 'sounding simplistic' is worth addressing briefly — it's a confidence issue, not a conceptual gap. I should push back on the simplicity concern and redirect toward the writing, since we've been circling long enough and the student has the ingredients they need.

exchanges.student: Okay, rough version: development still has some meaning if it’s about improving people’s lives, especially reducing poverty and building resilience, but it’s contested because different actors define success differently. I still feel like the crisis part is where I’ll go vague, and I’m trying a different angle now since the last few didn’t fully click for me.
exchanges.tutor: That's a good foundation — hold onto it. Now apply your own logic to the crisis: if different actors define success differently, do you think they'd also disagree about what *caused* the crisis? Who benefits from blaming markets, and who benefits from blaming government?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid rough thesis. I should affirm it clearly and briefly. They're now flagging the crisis question as their weak point, which is the third part of the prompt. Rather than letting them keep searching for a new angle, I should connect their existing argument (power defines success, markets vs. government) to the crisis question directly. This is a natural progression. I'll ask one targeted question to help them apply their own logic to the crisis question.

exchanges.student: Yeah, probably, but now it feels even more abstract. I think I need help making it less philosophical and more like an actual paper instead of just more questions.
exchanges.tutor: Fair point — let's ground it. You have an argument about poverty, resilience, and contested definitions. Can you think of one specific country or community that either fits or challenges your definition of development success? A concrete example will pull your paper out of the abstract.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated and feeling like the conversation has been too abstract. This is a fair signal — we've been in the conceptual phase long enough and they have enough building blocks. However, I cannot write for them. The right move is to push them toward grounding their abstract ideas in a concrete example, which will naturally make the paper feel more like a paper. I should also acknowledge their frustration without abandoning the Socratic method entirely. A concrete country or case will bridge the gap between philosophy and paper.

exchanges.student: Maybe Vietnam or Kerala, but I’m not confident enough to choose. I’m still pretty muddled, and that angle didn’t fully work for me either, so I’d rather get a really concise sample claim I can react to than keep guessing.
exchanges.tutor: I won't write a sample claim — that's the one thing you have to do yourself. But Vietnam and Kerala are both excellent, well-known examples in development literature. You don't need more confidence to pick one — you just need to pick. Which one feels more connected to your argument about poverty reduction and resilience? Commit to it and start writing. You have everything you need.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, this time a 'sample claim.' I must refuse clearly. However, I should note that the student has now mentioned two concrete examples (Vietnam and Kerala) — both are actually excellent and well-known in development literature. I should push back on their self-doubt and get them to commit to one. We've now had many exchanges and the student has all the ingredients: a thesis, a crisis angle, and now two potential examples. It's time to push them firmly toward writing rather than continuing to search for more scaffolding.
